result:
Time is a funny thing. We measure it in seconds, minutes, and hours, packaging it into neat little boxes that we can predictably cross off a calendar. Yet, our actual human experience of time is anything but linear. A miserable afternoon spent in a waiting room can stretch on for a seeming eternity, while a joyous year can vanish in the blink of an eye. We are constantly caught in a tug-of-war between the heavy weight of the past and the anxious uncertainty of the future, often forgetting that the only place we truly exist is right now.

Memories complicate this even further. They act as an emotional time machine, anchoring us to moments that have long since passed. The smell of rain on hot asphalt might suddenly transport you back to a childhood summer, making a memory feel as vivid as the room you are sitting in. But memories are fickle. They fade, warp, and rewrite themselves. We aren't really remembering the actual event; we are only remembering the last time we thought about it. It’s like a photocopy of a photocopy, slowly losing its original sharpness but gaining a hazy, nostalgic beauty in the process.

Perhaps that is why finding stillness is so difficult. To simply sit, breathe, and notice the exact moment you are in is a quiet rebellion against the relentless forward march of the clock. The present is fleeting, but it is also infinitely renewable—a quiet pause in the rushing river of our lives.
